coredata.dc:title: Facile and sensitive detection of dopamine based on in situ formation of fluorescent polydopamine nanoparticles catalyzed by peroxidase-like ficin
coredata.dc:creator: Pang, Yanjiao
Shi, Ying
Pan, Yadi
Yang, Yufang
Long, Yijuan
Zheng, Huzhi
coredata.dc:description: Polydopamine nanoparticles (pDA NPs), a kind of popular polymer material, have been broadly applied to various areas owing to its excellent physicochemical properties. In this work, we employed ficin as a peroxidase-mimic to in situ synthesize intrinsic fluorescent pDA NPs and developed a fluorescence turn-on method for rapid dopamine (DA) sensing which is based on monitoring the intrinsic fluorescence of pDA NPs. Ficin possesses intrinsic peroxidase-like activity that could catalyze the oxidization of dopamine to its quinone derivative and autopolymerize into fluorescent pDA NPs by H2O2. Thus, the fluorescence intensity of pDA NPs gradually enhanced with increasing concentration of DA. Under the optimum conditions, the strategy showed a good linear relationship from 10 nM to 5.0 μM (R2 = 0.996) and provided an exciting detection limit of 5.5 nM. More importantly, the presented sensing approach has high sensitivity, good selectivity and has been successfully applied to DA sensing in human serum samples. With its good analytical figures of merit, our proposed analytical method is promising for the detection of DA-related diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dopamine detection
Fluorescent polydopamine nanoparticles
Ficin
Peroxidase-like activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of DA in human blood samples
Feasibility and mechanism of the system for DA sensing
DA sensing based on the formation of fluorescent pDA NPs
DA sensing in human blood samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the formation of fluorescent pDA nanoparticles using ficin as a peroxidase mimic for DA sensing.

(a) The fluorescence emission spectra of (1) DA, (2) DA + ficin, (3) DA + H2O2 and (4) DA + H2O2 + ficin at 30 °C afer 150 min incubation. (b) EPR spectra of OH radicals in the system of DMPO, DMPO + H2O2, DMPO + H2O2 + ficin and DMPO + H2O2 + ficin + DA. (All the conditions: 0.40 mg mL−1 ficin, 40 μM H2O2, 5 μM DA, 0.20 M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0)).

(a) TEM images of the as-prepared pDA nanoparticles. (b) Absorption spectra of (1) DA, (2) DA + ficin, (3) DA + H2O2 and (4) DA + H2O2 + ficin. The inset photographs show the DA samples color changes under corresponding conditions. (All the conditions: 0.40 mg mL−1 ficin, 40 μM H2O2, 10 μM DA, 0.20 M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0), and 30 °C).

Effects of (a) pH, (b) concentration of H2O2, (c) temperature, and (d) incubation time on the detection of DA. Where F and F0 are fluorescence intensity at 476 nm in the presence and absence of 5.0 μM DA, respectively. The error bars represent standard deviations from three independent measurements.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the sensing system in the presence of DA with various concentrations. (b) Fluorescence responses of the ficin-H2O2-DA system toward DA (inset: the calibration curve of DA detection), where F and F0 are fluorescence intensity at 476 nm in the presence of different concentrations of DA and the absence of DA, respectively. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent experiments.

Selectivity of the DA assay. (a) Responses to different substances; (b) Effects of coexisting substances on the determination of DA. DA concentration is 5.0 μM and concentration of potential interfering species is 50 μM. Experimental conditions: 0.40 mg mL−1 ficin, 40 μM H2O2, 0.20 M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0), and 30 °C. The error bars represent standard deviation of measurements.
Detection results of DA in human blood samples.

head.ce:title: Facile and sensitive detection of dopamine based on in situ formation of fluorescent polydopamine nanoparticles catalyzed by peroxidase-like ficin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polydopamine nanoparticles (pDA NPs), a kind of popular polymer material, have been broadly applied to various areas owing to its excellent physicochemical properties. In this work, we employed ficin as a peroxidase-mimic to in situ synthesize intrinsic fluorescent pDA NPs and developed a fluorescence turn-on method for rapid dopamine (DA) sensing which is based on monitoring the intrinsic fluorescence of pDA NPs. Ficin possesses intrinsic peroxidase-like activity that could catalyze the oxidization of dopamine to its quinone derivative and autopolymerize into fluorescent pDA NPs by H2O2. Thus, the fluorescence intensity of pDA NPs gradually enhanced with increasing concentration of DA. Under the optimum conditions, the strategy showed a good linear relationship from 10 nM to 5.0 μM (R2 = 0.996) and provided an exciting detection limit of 5.5 nM. More importantly, the presented sensing approach has high sensitivity, good selectivity and has been successfully applied to DA sensing in human serum samples. With its good analytical figures of merit, our proposed analytical method is promising for the detection of DA-related diseases.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (3,4-dihydoxyphenethylamine, DA), a catecholamine neurotransmitter, mainly exists in the central nervous system of human and other mammals, which plays an important role in the nervous activity [1,2]. It can regulate many biological processes in the cardiovascular system and hormone secretion system as well as manage a variety of cognitive functions of central nervous system, such as memory, behavior, learning and emotion [3–7]. It has been reported that the abnormal concentration of DA in biological fluids is regarded as one of the indicators used in the diagnosis of several neurological diseases [8], including Schizophrenia [9], Parkinson’s diseases [10], restless leg syndrome [11], and Alzheimer’s diseases [12]. Besides, these diseases attract considerable attentions due to the physiological and pathophysiological effect of DA levels. Therefore, it is essential to develop a sensitive and selective method that can efficiently detect DA. In the past few years, a variety of available analytical techniques have been developed and devoted to DA detection, such as electrochemical analysis [13], capillary electrophoresis [14], chromatography coupled with spectroscopy [15], colorimetric method [16] and fluorescent probes [17]. Dismayingly, most of the above methods generally suffer from time-consuming, tedious or lengthy procedures, and relatively high cost. Accordingly, a facile and sensitive method for the determination of DA is highly desirable for researching human physiological functions, which was propitious to achieve clinical diagnosing of neurological diseases.

DA, as generally known, is susceptible to be oxidized to its quinone derivative and then self-polymerizes to form pDA NPs under oxidative stress or alkaline environment [18,19]. With the merits of excellent physicochemical properties, pDA NPs have attracted a great deal of application to various areas [20–24]. Very recently, it has been reported that pDA has fluorescent properties under certain conditions. For instance, Zhang et al. reported pDA NPs that obtained by treating DA with concentrated H2O2 possess fluorescence, which can be applied to cellular imaging [25]. Up to now, however, the development of the intrinsically fluorescent pDA NPs for biosensing application was rarely explored. As far as we know, only Bayindirz et al. reported the formation of fluorescent pDA NPs under alkaline conditions, which is further used for the sensing of DA [26] and Chu et al. reported the fluorescent pDA NPs are catalytically synthetized by Fe3O4 nanozyme and its application in detection of Zn2+ [27]. Therefore, it is quite indispensable to further explore pDA NPs with intrinsic fluorescence for biosensing application.

Ficin (EC 3.4.22.3), a cysteine proteolytic enzyme, mainly presents in the latex of ficus trees, playing pivotal roles in many plant physiological processes and applying to a variety of industries such as food and pharmaceutical [28–30]. Recently, our group has found that ficin exhibited intrinsic peroxidase-like activity, which can catalyze H2O2 decomposition into OH radicals that are a powerful oxidizing agent [31]. On this basis, we developed a new method for DA sensing based on the in situ formation of fluorescent pDA NPs by catalyzing the oxidation of DA in the presence of H2O2 with the aid of the peroxidase-like activity of ficin. The conceptual framework of this method for DA sensing was shown in Scheme 1 . In the presence of ficin, H2O2 can be decomposed into OH radicals that are able to rapidly oxidize dopamine to its quinone derivative and then auto polymerizes into fluorescent pDA NPs. Meanwhile, within a certain concentration range the fluorescence intensity of pDA NPs gradually enhanced with increasing concentration of DA. The sensing approach was verified to be effective for the detection of DA in human serum samples, which provides a broad prospect in practical applications. Furthermore, this study gives a new insight on the application of intrinsic fluorescent pDA NPs to develop a facile and sensitive biosensing system.

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%), acetic acid, sodium acetate, glucose and sucrose were purchased from Chongqing Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. (Chongqing, China). Dopamine hydrochloride (DA), ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), gallic acid (GA), urea, glutamic acid (Glu), glycine (Gly), aspartic acid (Asp), lysine (Lys), alanine (Ala) and phenylalanine (Phe) were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China), premium grade ficin (F4165, ≥0.1 U mg−1) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). All other chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water purified with a Milli-Q system (18.2 MΩ, Millipore) was used in all runs.

The fluorescence measurements were conducted on an F-4500 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The excitation wavelength was set at 400 nm, the Ex and Em slits were set at 10.0 and 10.0 nm, respectively, with a 700 V PMT voltage. The absorption spectra were recorded by a UV–2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a JEM-2100 instrument (JEOL Ltd., Japan). All ESR measurements were carried out using a JES-FA200 Spectrometer (JEOL, Japan) at an ambient temperature.

0.20 mL of freshly prepared DA aqueous solution with concentrations from 0 to 50 μM, 0.20 mL of 4.0 mg mL−1 ficin, 0.20 mL of 0.40 mM H2O2, 1.0 mL of 0.20 M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0), and 0.40 mL H2O were introduced into 2.0 mL eppendorf tube, and incubated for 150 min at 30 °C to form fluorescent pDA NPs. And then, the fluorescence spectra of the reaction solution were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA in human blood samples
ce:section.ce:para: The human blood sample permitted and obtained from healthy volunteers was collected in a heparin anticoagulated tube. Furthermore, the volunteers’ consent and approval from the Institutional Research Ethics Committee of Southwest University hospital were obtained for research purposes. And then, the blood samples were treated according to previous literatures [32]. Briefly, the collected samples were first treated by centrifugal ultrafiltration using a filter (cutoff is 1 kDa) at 3000 rpm for 15 min at room temperature to remove haemocytes. The detection of DA in human samples was carried out by well-established spiked method. Firstly, the aliquot (0.20 mL) of the filtrates were spiked with 0.20 mL of different concentrations standard DA solution. Then, 0.20 mL of 4.0 mg mL−1 ficin, 0.20 mL of 0.40 mM H2O2, and 0.2 mL H2O were added into above reaction solution containing 1.0 mL of 0.20 M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0), and incubated for 150 min at 30 °C. The subsequent operations and fluorescence measurements were performed in the same as described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and mechanism of the system for DA sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The oxidation of DA by H2O2 using DNAzymes [33] or nanozymes [34] was reported by some groups. However, only a few papers mentioned the fluorescent properties of the oxidation products. Therefore, in this work, we constructed a novel fluorescence sensing platform for detecting DA based on the in situ formation of fluorescent pDA NPs via catalyzing the oxidation of DA in the presence of H2O2 by the peroxidase-like activity of ficin. To demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed strategy for sensing DA, fluorescence spectra were recorded. As shown in Fig. 1 a, a remarkable fluorescence signal was observed when DA was mixed with both ficin and H2O2 (curve 4), while barely any fluorescence signal was generated in the presence of free DA, DA with ficin alone and DA mixed with H2O2 alone (curve 1–3). It is noteworthy that we used rather low concentration of H2O2 during the synthesis compared to the previously reported method [35]. All the above results indicated that ficin can successfully catalyze the oxidation of DA by H2O2 into quinone and initiate the polymerization reaction to form fluorescent pDA NPs that showed an excitation wavelength of 400 nm and the maximum emission peak at 476 nm. To prove the catalytic mechanism of ficin, a series of electron spin resonance (ESR) experiments with 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin trap was used to confirm the generation of the OH radicals. From Fig. 1b, one can see that there are no apparent OH radical signals in the systems of DMPO and DMPOH2O2. In contrast, the ESR spectrum in the system of DMPOH2O2-ficin displayed a characteristic peak of the typical DMPO/OH spin adduct signal, demonstrating that ficin converts H2O2 to OH radical [36]. However, after the addition of DA to this solution, the typical DMPO/OH spin adduct signal intensity is obviously weakened, clearly indicating that the generated OH radicals are consumed owing to rapidly oxidize DA into fluorescent pDA NPs.

Additionally, as displayed in Fig. S1a, we also found that the emission intensity initially enhanced with increasing excitation wavelength from 360 nm to 400 nm and then began to decrease gradually from 410 nm to 430 nm. As shown in Fig. S1b, with the increasing excitation wavelength, the emission peak shifted accordingly to the longer wavelength, illustrating its excitation-dependent fluorescent property, which is similar to the fluorescence spectra of dopamine oligomers synthesized in an acidic condition via a hydrothermal method [37].

In order to further prove fluorescent pDA NPs formed by ficin catalyzing the oxidation of DA in the presence of H2O2, the obtained pDA NPs were characterized by TEM and UV–vis absorption spectra. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the TEM image of prepared pDA NPs displayed irregular shape. Particle size distribution of pDA NPs is given in Fig. S2, which shows the broad size distribution ranging from few tens of nanometers to hundreds of nanometers. The reason was ascribed to the interaction of different oligomeric units [38]. In contrast, the TEM image of DA mixed with H2O2 is almost invisible in the form of the pDA NPs (Fig. S3), demonstrating that H2O2 does not oxidize DA to produce pDA NPs at rather low concentration under the same conditions. In addition, when ficin was added into DA solution in the presence of H2O2, the maximum absorption peak centered at 450 nm (curve 4) was obtained in Fig. 2b and a brown color was observed after incubation 150 min at 30 °C (inset of Fig. 2b). However, control samples of free DA, DA with ficin alone and DA mixed with H2O2 alone have no color change or absorption peak in the visible region (curve 1–3). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of pDA NPs over time was also examined in Fig. S4, which displayed outstanding stability within a week, indicating that it is advantageous to bioanalysis application.

To achieve a high sensitivity, we explored the effect of various experimental conditions such as ficin concentration, the pH of the catalytic reaction, H2O2 concentration, incubation temperature and reaction time on DA sensing. Ficin plays a key role in dopamine oxidization owing to its high peroxidase-like activity for producing fluorescent pDA. As shown in Fig. S5, it was found that the highest fluorescence intensity was obtained when the concentration of ficin was 0.40 mg mL−1. The effect of pH on the DA sensing was shown in Fig. 3 a, we can observe that fluorescence intensity initially increased with an increase in pH up to 4.0, however decreased as the pH was continue to increased, indicating that ficin had the highest efficiency at pH 4.0, which is almost consistent with the peroxidase-like activity of ficin previously reported by our group [31]. The influence of H2O2 concentration on DA detection was studied in the range of 0–100 μM, and fluorescence intensity increased with an increasing H2O2 concentration in the range from 0 to 40 μM, subsequently, maintained almost constant when the concentration of H2O2 was higher than 40 μM (Fig. 3b). So, the optimal H2O2 concentration was chosen to 40 μM.

In addition, we carried out the reaction at different temperatures, the temperature −dependent response curve was shown in Fig. 3c. The result suggested that the highest activity was exhibited at 30 °C, however, at even higher temperature, the fluorescence intensity diminished gradually, possibly due to decomposition of H2O2. The reaction time of oxidization and ploymerization reactions was also an important parameter, as shown in Fig. 3d, the fluorescence intensity increased gradually with reaction time scale from 0 to 90 min, then slowly tended to level off. Thus, 150 min was selected as the optimized reaction time for the subsequent experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DA sensing based on the formation of fluorescent pDA NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized conditions, the ability of the proposed sensing system for quantitative determination of DA was investigated by performing the assays with a series of various concentrations of DA. As shown in Fig. 4 a, there was an obvious increase in the fluorescence intensity of the solutions with increasing DA concentration from 0 to 8.0 μM. Upon further increasing the DA concentration, the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually, which might be due to the quenching impact of excessive DA and the formation of large-sized pDA NPs [39]. A good linear curve was obtained between the changes of fluorescence intensity (F-F0 ) at 476 nm and the concentration of DA over the range of 10 nM to 5.0 μM (Fig. 4b). The regression equation was F-F0 = 1203[DA]-8.612, R2 = 0.996. The limit of detection of the suggested assay was calculated to 5.5 nM (LOD = 3σ/k method, in which σ means the standard deviation of blank experiment (n = 11) and k means the slope of calibration curve), which was lower than many previous reports (Table S1 in the Supplementary material). In addition, the relative standard deviations (RSD) in the F-F0 value of three independent experiments are between 2.6% and 6.3%, revealing that our method has excellent reproducibility in detecting DA. The above results signified that the developed strategy has enormous potential for highly sensitive sensing of DA.

In order to evaluate the specificity and selectivity of the developed approach, the sensing system was treated with other possible interfering chemicals in serum including ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), urea, glucose, sucrose, gallic acid (GA), glutamic acid (Glu), glycine (Gly), aspartic acid (Asp), lysine (Lys), alanine (Ala) and phenylalanine (Phe) for comparison under the same conditions (Fig. 5 a). The interference studies showed that as-developed assay exhibited a conspicuous increase of fluorescence intensity in the presence of 5.0 μM DA, while no fluorescence enhancement was observed even at 10-fold of all the investigated interferents, indicating satisfactory selectivity of the method. The desirable selectivity of the DA sensing system is ascribed to pDA NPs formation only in the presence of DA.

To further assess the selectivity of the present method, a coexisting experiment for DA detection was performed with the above potential interferences as the coexisting substances. It could be observed from Fig. 5b that even in the presence of ten-fold of interference substances, the sensing platform still worked the same for DA detection, i.e., the relative error caused by the interfering species was less than ±5%. Thus, the result further illustrated that the potential existing interference did not interrupt the accurate test of DA, which laid a solid foundation for the detection of DA in complicated biological fluids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DA sensing in human blood samples
ce:section.ce:para: Taking advantage of selectivity and sensitivity of the proposed system, the feasibility of this sensing platform for DA detecting in human blood samples was validated by using the standard addition method. The assay results shown in Table 1 , the recovery values ranging from 92.5% to 110% were obtained, and the RSD values were less than 8.31%. These results revealed that the developed pDA NPs-based method had acceptable accuracy and reproducibility for DA analysis in real samples.

In summary, we have developed a novel approach for DA sensing based on measuring the fluorescence signal of pDA NPs which were in situ formed by catalyzing the oxidation of DA in the presence of H2O2 with the aid of the peroxidase-like activity of ficin. The developed approach for DA sensing mainly relied on the fact that ficin possess intrinsic peroxidase-like activity that can quickly oxidize DA into its quinone derivative and autopolymerize into fluorescent pDA NPs. The proposed method was proven to be extremely simple, rapid and cost-effective. Furthermore, it also showed good sensing performance for DA assay with high sensitivity and desirable selectivity over other possible interferences existing in human serum, which provides a potential platform for the detection of DA related diseases.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21405124, 21175110) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Nos. XDJK2013A022, XDJK2014C173, XDJK2017D052).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.02.128.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A unique turn-off fluorescent strategy for sensing dopamine based on formed polydopamine (pDA) using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as fluorescent probe
Silver nanoprobe for sensitive and selective colorimetric detection of dopamine via robust Ag–catechol interaction
On the pH-dependent quenching of quantum dot photoluminescence by redox active dopamine
Design and synthesis of fluorescent reagents for selective detection of dopamine
A biosensor based on gold nanoparticles stabilized in poly(allylamine hydrochloride) and decorated with laccase for determination of dopamine
Fabrication of 1, 4-bis (aminomethyl)benzene and cobalt hydroxide@graphene oxide for selective detection of dopamine in the presence of ascorbic acid and serotonin
Dopamine and oxytocin interactions underlying behaviors: potential contributions to behavioral disorders
Preparation of quantum dots CdTe decorated graphene composite for sensitive detection of uric acid and dopamine
One-pot synthesis of boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as the fluorescence probe for dopamine based on the redox reaction between Cr (VI) and dopamine
Intertemporal choice in Parkinson’s disease and restless legs syndrome
Recent progress in development of dopamine receptor subtype-selective agents: potential therapeutics for neurological and psychiatric disorders
Sensitive and selective electrochemical detection of dopamine using an electrode modified with carboxylated carbonaceous spheres
A multichannel native fluorescence detection system for capillary electrophoretic analysis of neurotransmitters in single neurons
Determination of dopamine and serotonin in human urine samples utilizing microextraction online with liquid chromatography/electrospray tandem mass spectrometry
Synthesis of nitrogen-and iron-containing carbon dots, and their application to colorimetric and fluorometric determination of dopamine
Carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal treatment of dopamine as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for the label-free detection of iron (III) ions and dopamine
MnO2-induced synthesis of fluorescent polydopamine nanoparticles for reduced glutathione sensing in human whole blood
Monitoring dopamine quinone-induced dopaminergic neurotoxicity using dopamine functionalized quantum dots
Polydopamine and its derivative materials: synthesis and promising applications in energy, environmental, and biomedical fields
Facile deposition of gold nanoparticles on core-shell Fe3O4@polydopamine as recyclable nanocatalyst
Polydopamine-derived porous nanofibers as host of ZnFe2O4 nanoneedles: towards high-performance anodes for lithium-ion batteries
Biocompatible polydopamine-encapsulated gadolinium-loaded carbon nanotubes for MRI and color mapping guided photothermal dissection of tumor metastasis
Polydopamine-reinforced magnetization of zeolitic imidazolate framework ZIF-7 for magnetic solid-phase extraction of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons from the air-water environment
Biocompatible polydopamine fluorescent organic nanoparticles: facile preparation and cell imaging
Turn-on fluorescent dopamine sensing based on in situ formation of visible light emitting polydopamine nanoparticles
Iron oxide nanozyme catalyzed synthesis of fluorescent polydopamine for light-up Zn 2+ detection
Ficin-catalyzed asymmetric aldol reactions of heterocyclic ketones with aldehydes
Purification characterization, and solvent-induced thermal stabilization of ficin from Ficus carica
Autolysis control and structural changes of purified ficin from Iranian fig latex with synthetic inhibitors
A FRET-based carbon dot?MnO2 nanosheet architecture for glutathione sensing in human whole blood samples
Nucleoapzymes: hemin/G-quadruplex DNAzyme–aptamer binding site conjugates with superior enzyme-like catalytic functions
Mimicking horseradish peroxidase functions using Cu2+-modified carbon nitride nanoparticles or Cu2+-modified carbon dots as heterogeneous catalysts
Biocompatible polydopamine fluorescent organic nanoparticles: facile preparation and cell imaging
Mechanism of the oxidation of 3,5,3′,5′ −tetramethylbenzidine by myeloperoxidase determined by transient-and steady-state kinetics
Oxidative self-polymerization of dopamine in an acidic environment
Novel photoelectrochemical immunosensor for disease-related protein assisted by hemin/G-quadruplex-based DNAzyme on gold nanoparticles to enhance cathodic photocurrent on p-CuBi2O4 semiconductor
In situ synthesis of fluorescent polydopamine nanoparticles coupled with enzyme-controlled dissolution of MnO2 nanoflakes for a sensitive immunoassay of cancer biomarkers

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yanjiao Pang received her B.Sc. degree in chemistry at Mianyang Normal University in 2015. Now, she is a graduate student in the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Southwestern University of China. Her research interest focused on molecular spectrum analysis.

Ying Shi received her B.Sc. degree in chemistry at Datong University in 2013. Now she is a doctoral student in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University of China. Her major research interest is molecular spectrum analysis.

Yidi Pan received her B.Sc. degree in chemistry at Chongqing Normal University in 2016. Now, she is a graduate student in the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Southwestern University of China. Her research interest focused on molecular spectrum analysis.
Yufang Yang received her M.S. degree in chemistry at Southwest University in 2017. Her major research interest is molecular spectrum analysis.

Yijuan Long received her Ph.D. degree in chemistry at Southwest University in 2013. Now she is a senior experimentalist in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. Her research interest is spectrum analysis.

Huzhi Zheng* received his Ph.D. degree in chemistry at Wuhan University in 2005. Now he is a professor in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, China. His main research interests lie in drug delivery and molecular spectrum analysis.
